content.full_text:
HARVARD UNIVERSITY
JOHN F KENNEDY SCHOOL OF GOVERNMENT
CAMBRIDGE, MASSACHUSETTS 02138

PROGRAM FOR SENIOR EXECUTIVES
IN STATE AND LOCAL GOVERNMENT
(617) 495-1165


Dear Public Official:

Harvard University offers an intensive three week executive management
program for executives from the highest levels of state and local
government. The Program for Senior Executives in State and Local
Government is Harvard's major developmental effort for senior
level managers from state, municipal and county government, and their
elected counterparts.

The Program's curriculum is designed to build on the significant
management skills which experienced executives already possess. It
focuses on several key areas: organizational strategy, political
management, policy development, management control and operations, and the
management of human resources. The diversity of the participant mix and
the sharing of management perspectives among senior executives nationwide
make for a challenging experience.

Since 1979, more than 1200 senior public officials from 47 states have
attended this program. I encourage you to share the enclosed brochure
describing the Program for Senior Executives in State and Local Government
with your colleagues. I urge you to consider attending the 1990 session.
Please feel free to call me at 617/495-1165.

We look forward to hearing from you.

Sincerely,

Elizabeth C. Nill
Director


Enclosed: Brochure

HARVARD
UNIVERSITY

Program for Senior Exercises
in State and Local Government
John R Kennedy School of Government
June 10-29, 1990 and July 8-27, 1990

The Program

Senior officials today are under constant pressure to deliver public services more efficiently and effectively. Operating in an environment where authority to act is diffused, public managers are still responsible and accountable for results. At the same time, the challenges of day-to-day management leave little time for reflection, creativity, or assessment of personal management skills and needs.

Harvard University's Program for Senior Executives in State and Local Government offers an intensive three weeks of executive education for key public managers. This rigorous residential program is designed to prepare participants for increased responsibilities, to stimulate and "recharge" interest in new management ideas and techniques, and to develop relationships among public sector colleagues across the country.

Participants may attend one of two sessions offered June 10-29, 1990, and July 8-27, 1990.

At these sessions the John F. Kennedy School of Government brings together senior public managers in an interactive environment with a faculty that combines academic expertise and practical public sector experience. The Program provides a unique opportunity for public executives to:

• Sharpen their problem-solving and analytic skills.

• Gain insight into the political, social, and economic forces that shape their operational environments.

• Examine their ethical and professional responsibilities.

• Understand the role of public executives in improving productivity and managing human resources.

• Exchange perspectives with experienced faculty and participants of diverse backgrounds.

Participants return to their organizations with a keener ability to define problems and analyze policy options, a broader perspective on their roles, and a renewed pride in the value of public service.

The State and Local Program's curriculum sharpens a participant's understanding of the broad responsibilities and mandates of government. Rather than focusing on narrow technical matters, the Program is designed to strengthen the participant's overall capacity to act as an effective general manager.

Organized and presented by an interdisciplinary team of Harvard faculty, the curriculum offers the opportunity to refine skills in the following areas of management responsibility:

Strategy and Political Management Managers have the central task of establishing meaningful, attainable objectives for their organizations in light of the external pressures they confront. The curriculum includes discussion of the tools available for influencing and/or controlling the behavior and performance of entities beyond the manager's own agency. It examines strategic interaction with staff, peers, and supervisors; elected and appointed officials; and the press and citizen groups.

Policy Analysis and Design An effective leader must make decisions based on a broad understanding of philosophic and economic principles. The curriculum investigates the process of developing and evaluating policy alternatives for achieving goals, including the role of quantitative methods.

Mobilizing Organizational Capacity A key element of the manager's job is to assemble the resources necessary to implement chosen goals. The Program considers how managers organize and deploy their resources, develop systems for control and information, and develop a workforce committed to providing public services in an effective and efficient manner. Included is consideration of options for improving productivity, using information, and more effectively managing human resources.

The curriculum is based largely on
the case method, pioneered for use in
executive education at Harvard University. This approach actively engages the
participants in the classroom and both
requires and elicits a high level of
intellectual inquiry. Cases are based on
actual problems that have confronted
public managers, and participants are
often asked to take the role of key
decision-makers.

Participants work each evening to
prepare their cases for the next day,
identifying basic problems and relevant
facts, and formulating alternative
solutions. Small discussion groups meet
each morning to provide a forum for

testing ideas and recommendations. The
discussion continues in class where
participants, guided by the instructor,
seek to develop an understanding both of
the specific case and of its more general
implications. Instructors typically
provide a framework for the class to develop
an analytic framework within which to
understand the discussion that has just
taken place.

While the schedule is rigorous and
demanding, there are special occasions
that change the pace for an afternoon or
evening. Traditional Program events
include a tour of and reception at the
John F. Kennedy Library, and an
evening at Fenway Park with the Red

Sox or at Symphony Hall with the
Boston Pops Orchestra.

Refresher Seminar Kennedy
School alumni from all executive
education and academic degree programs are invited each year to return to
the School for an educational program
designed to keep them abreast of areas
of immediate and pressing concern. The
focus of the seminar changes each year
to reflect topics of current interest. The
three day program includes special State
and Local Program events, as well as an
opportunity to interact with faculty and
alumni from other Kennedy School
Programs. The next seminar will be
offered in April 1990.

Participants

The Program is especially designed for senior-level executives from
state, county, and municipal government, and their elected counterparts. In
the past, leaders of private, nonprofit organizations which provide major public
sector services, federal officials responsible for intergovernmental activities,
members of the media covering state and
local government, and corporate
executives with an interest in state and
local government have also found participation in the Program extremely
valuable.

One of the strengths of the State
and Local Program is the diversity of its
participants. Typical among recent
participants are:

• A cabinet secretary of a traditionally strong social service department
faced with the demands of managing a
much higher proportion of the service
delivery system through a private
contractor.

• The city manager of a southern
state capital struggling to reconcile
service demands of older neighborhoods
and new developments.

• A newly elected state treasurer
dealing with sensitive personnel problems inherited from the previous
administration.

• A city councilor of a major
southwestern city making decisions on a
$300 million budget with virtually no
personal staff.

Because all of the participants are
called upon to contribute their professional expertise to complement the
learning experience, each session's
members are carefully selected to reflect
a broad representation of functional
responsibilities; level of government;
and geographic, ethnic, and racial
diversity.

Environment

The John F. Kennedy School of Government is Harvard University's professional school that serves the public sector much as the Schools of Business, Law, and Medicine serve their respective professions. The School's efforts to provide leadership for the public sector have led to a comprehensive series of initiatives, including graduate degree and executive education programs, major research centers, and public affairs conferences.

The Kennedy School houses: the Center for Business and Government, the Center for Science and International Affairs, the Energy and Environmental Policy Center, the Malcolm Wiener Center for Social Policy, the Taubman Center for State and Local Government, and the Joan Shorenstein Barone Center on the Press, Politics and Public Policy. The Institute of Politics bridges the School and the world of elective politics by bringing to the campus leading political figures.

A major attraction of the Program is its location on the Harvard campus in Cambridge, just across the Charles River from Boston. Participants may take advantage of an impressive array of cultural, social, and athletic resources available at the University, including the world's largest university library system, fine museums and galleries, musical and theatrical productions, and exceptional athletic facilities.

Harvard Square, a short walk from the School, offers a unique collection of bookstores, boutiques, coffee houses, bars, and restaurants, catering to a variety of tastes and styles.

Boston, just a few minutes away by subway, is a city of historical and cultural significance. A walk along the Freedom Trail, an afternoon browsing in Quincy Market, outdoor symphony concerts on the Esplanade, and the Red Sox at Fenway Park are just a few of the many diversions it provides for its permanent and temporary residents.

On weekends the beaches of the North Shore, Cape Cod, and the Islands lie within easy driving distance.

The Faculty

Kennedy School faculty who have
taught in the Program:
Alan Altshuler, Ruth and Frank
Stanton Professor of Urban Policy,
Planning and Design and director of the
Taubman Center for State and Local
Government. He has been dean of the
Graduate School of Public Administration at New York University and a
professor of political science and urban
planning at MIT.
Julie Wilson, Lecturer in Public
Policy, is interested in family policy,
economic development, welfare policy,
and survey research methodology. She
recently returned from three years at the
New York State Department of Social
Services, where she directed the Office
of Program Planning, Analysis, and
Development.
Linda Kaboolian, Adjunct
Lecturer in Public Policy, director of the
Harvard Trade Union Program and
Research Fellow at the School's Center
for Business and Government. In New
York State, she was a staff member to
the lieutenant governor and a lobbyist
for public higher education. She has
also served several terms as an elected
executive officer of a public employee
union and was their chief bargainer.
Herman (Dutch) Leonard,
George F. Baker, Jr. Professor of Public
Management. A member of the Alaska
Governor's Council on Economic
Policy, he participates in task forces on

infrastructure funding for the U.S.
Senate Budget Committee, the Governmental Accounting Standards Board, and
the Massachusetts Governor's Advisory
Commission.
Richard J. Light, Professor of
Education, has chaired advisory panels
for four U.S. executive agencies and has
worked on national evaluations of day
care, job training, early education, and
school effectiveness.
Martin A. Linsky, Lecturer in
Public Policy. Former assistant minority
floor leader of the Massachusetts House
of Representatives, assistant attorney
general for Massachusetts, editor of The
Real Paper, and editorial writer for the
Boston Globe.
Jose A. Gómez-Ibáñez, Professor
of Public Policy and Urban Planning.
Former senior staff economist with the
President's Council of Economic
Advisers. Consultant to several public
agencies including the U.S. Department

of Transportation, the U.S. Interstate
Commerce Commission, and the
Ministry of Finance, Republic of
Singapore.
Robert B. Reich, Lecturer in
Public Policy. Formerly director of
policy planning at the Federal Trade
Commission, he has also served as an
assistant solicitor general in the Justice
Department. He is the author of several
books and articles on economic development, regulation, antitrust, public
management, and international trade.
Marc J. Roberts, Professor of
Political Economy and Health Policy at
the School and Harvard's School of
Public Health and Faculty Chair of the
State and Local Program. Former
chairman of the American Association
for the Advancement of Science Committee on Environmental Alteration.
Consultant to various state and federal
health and environmental agencies.

General Information

Admission: The deadline for
postmarking application and sponsor's
form is Friday, March 30. A
candidate's admission will be deter-
mined by the nature of his or her current
responsibilities, substantive achieve-
ments and challenges faced, and
potential for future contribution.
Since the sharing of experiences is
critical to the Program's objectives,
participants will be chosen to reflect
diversity of backgrounds, geographic
regions, and types of agencies. If
several well-qualified applicants have
very similar backgrounds, one or more
candidates may be deferred to a future
class.

As a matter of law, policy and in-
stitutional commitment, Harvard
University does not discriminate among
applicants on the basis of race, religion,
sex, age, national origin, or handicap.

Sponsorship: The Admissions
Committee considers the sponsor's form
to confirm the organization's belief in
the applicant's management abilities and
potential.

Full sponsorship requires the
agency to pay the Program fee plus the
participant's ongoing salary. It also
entails freeing the individual of all
official responsibilities during the
Program. No application will be
considered until a sponsor's form has
been received.

Tuition and Fellowships: The
fee of $6,800 covers tuition, lodging,
meals, and all instructional materials.
Also included is full use of Harvard's
athletic, health, library, and cultural
facilities when available for use.

In cases where the applicant's
employer is financially unable to
provide full sponsorship, the applicant
is urged to seek financial support from
independent sources. Local founda-
tions and philanthropic organizations
have sponsored many past partici-
pants.

The Program offers a limited
number of full and partial fellowships
to applicants from particular geo-
graphic areas. It has a limited number
of general purpose fellowships as
well. Applicants who seek further
information should request it from the
Program.

Accommodations and
Services: Participants are housed in
executive program apartments
adjacent to the Harvard Business
School overlooking the Charles River.

Meals are provided from Monday
breakfast through Friday dinner each
week, plus breakfast and lunch the first
Saturday.

All participants, including those
from the immediate vicinity, are
required to reside on campus in order
to derive maximum benefit from daily
interaction with colleagues and the
faculty. Lodging for spouses or family
members is not available.

For Further Information:

Program for Senior Executives
in State and
Local Government
John F. Kennedy School of
Government
Harvard University
79 John F. Kennedy St.
Cambridge, MA 02138
Telephone: 617/495-1165
